name: Angolar

ancestors.notes: Portuguese superstrate: thinner than other Gulf of Guinea creoles. Angolar descended from escaped enslaved Africans (primarily Kimbundu/Mbundu speakers) who fled into the interior of São Tomé in the 16th century; less contact with Portuguese colonizers.
São Tomé Portuguese adstrate via ongoing contact with the dominant variety of the island.

notes: Angolar (Nga). Portuguese-based Gulf of Guinea creole; spoken in the southern coast of São Tomé island by the Angolares, descendants of escaped enslaved Africans. ~5,000 speakers. Unique within the Gulf of Guinea creole cluster: much stronger Bantu substrate, lexical tone system, and thinner Portuguese superstrate. KEY FEATURES: (1) LEXICAL TONE: Angolar is tonal (H/L), unusual among Atlantic creoles and unique in the GGC cluster; tone not conventionally marked in writing. (2) INTERDENTAL FRICATIVES: digraphs th=[θ], dh=[ð] (Maurer 1995; APiCS ch.36 gives the inventory /p b t d k g f v θ ð l m n ɲ j w/). /θ ð/ vs /s z/ are in near-complementary distribution — [s z] before /i/, [θ ð] before other vowels — so the orthographic ⟨th dh⟩ map faithfully to the interdentals. The primary/conservative reading is [θ]/[ð]; a Santomense-contact / younger-speaker variant reads plain stops [t]/[d] (Lorenzino 1998), recorded as the second grapheme value but not the default. (3) NO LABIAL-VELAR STOPS: earlier drafts listed gb=[ɡ͡b], kp=[k͡p], but APiCS ch.36 (Maurer) documents none in the inventory — the substrate is Bantu (Kimbundu) plus Edoid, not Kwa — so they are removed. (4) PRENASALIZED STOPS: mb/nd/ng are prenasalized in all positions (word-initial and intervocalic alike; APiCS ch.36), so ⟨ng⟩ defaults to [ŋɡ] except in a word-final coda where it is the plain velar nasal [ŋ]. (5) CIRCUMFLEX = VOWEL QUALITY, NOT NASALIZATION: ⟨â ê î ô û⟩ mark close-mid / peripheral oral vowels (ê=[e], ô=[o]) within the seven-vowel system a/e/ɛ/i/o/ɔ/u, NOT phonemic nasal vowels — an earlier draft nasalized them, which both blind judges and the sources contradict. Angolar does have a small, infrequent phonemic nasal-vowel set plus optional nasalization spread from an adjacent nasal, but these are not triggered by the circumflex. (6) HEAVY BANTU VOCABULARY: more Kimbundu-derived vocabulary than any other PT creole. Sources: Maurer (1995) is the primary linguistic description.

sources.author: Maurer, P.
sources.title: L'Angolar: Un Créole Afro-Portugais parlé à São Tomé
sources.notes: Definitive grammar; includes full phonological description with tone

sources.author: Hagemeijer, T.
sources.title: The Gulf of Guinea creoles: genetic and typological relations
sources.publisher: Journal of Pidgin and Creole Languages 24(1)

sources.author: Maurer, P.
sources.title: Angolar structure dataset (survey chapter)
sources.publisher: Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures Online (APiCS), ch. 36
sources.notes: Consonant inventory /p b t d k g f v θ ð l m n ɲ j w/ with no labial-velar stops; prenasalized stops in all positions; interdentals θ/ð vs s/z in near-complementary distribution.

sources.author: Lorenzino, G. A.
sources.title: The Angolar Creole Portuguese of São Tomé: Its Grammar and Sociolinguistic History
sources.publisher: LINCOM Europa (PhD, City University of New York)
sources.notes: Documents the Santomense-contact / younger-speaker variant in which the interdentals are read as plain stops [t]/[d].
